title: South Korea
practicalNote: Cards are convenient in most big-city places, but keep some KRW cash for markets, small purchases, and transit-card top-ups. For metro and buses, use Tmoney, EZL, WOWPASS, or Climate Card; on buses, tap when boarding and leaving if you need transfer handling. Outside tourist areas, keep the Korean name of your address or station handy.

title: Bosnia and Herzegovina
practicalNote: Bosnia and Herzegovina uses the convertible mark (KM/BAM); euros are not a dependable day-to-day payment plan. In Sarajevo you will mostly see Latin script, while Bosnian, Croatian, and Serbian are officially used.

title: Serbia
practicalNote: Serbia uses dinars; cards are convenient in many larger places, but keep some cash for markets, small purchases, and taxis. In Belgrade you will see both Cyrillic and Latin scripts.

title: Montenegro
practicalNote: Cards are common in tourist areas, but keep euro cash for small services and beach stops. Montenegrin is the official language; Serbian, Bosnian, Albanian, and Croatian are also in official use. Check buses and beach-area trips close to the day you travel.
